raw_response:
{
    "internal_reasoning": "This artwork is undoubtedly Edvard Munch's 'The Scream,' specifically the 1895 pastel on board version. This identification is confirmed by the visual evidence: the intense, vibrant red and orange hues of the sky (distinct from the darker 1893 tempera version), the specific textural quality of the pastel strokes, and the signature 'E Munch 1893' in the lower left corner. It is a well-documented nuance that Munch dated this 1895 pastel '1893,' aligning it with the genesis of the composition, despite it being executed two years later. This version is widely considered by scholars and the market to be the most aesthetically striking of the four versions due to its vivid coloration and the expressive freedom afforded by the pastel medium.\n\nArt-historically, this piece is a cornerstone of Expressionism and modern art. It captures the existential angst of the fin de siècle, translating internal psychological turmoil into external landscape distortion. The skeletal figure, the blood-red sky (often theorized to be inspired by the 1883 Krakatoa eruption), and the isolating perspective create an image of universal human anxiety. Its cultural resonance is unparalleled; it is likely one of the top five most recognizable images in global history, sitting alongside the Mona Lisa and Starry Night.\n\nRegarding valuation, we must look at the specific market history of this object. This exact work (the 1895 pastel) was sold at Sotheby's New York in May 2012 for $119,922,500, setting a record for the artist and for any work of art at auction at that time. To arrive at a current fair market value, we must adjust for over a decade of inflation (roughly 35-40% cumulative since 2012) and the appreciation of 'trophy' assets. While the broader art market has seen fluctuations, the ultra-high-end sector for masterpiece Impressionist and Modern art has remained robust, with works by Modigliani and Picasso achieving prices in the $170M–$180M range in private and auction settings in the mid-2010s. \n\nFurthermore, the prompt notes this is the 'only one in private hands.' The other three versions are held by Norwegian institutions (The National Museum and The Munch Museum in Oslo), making this the sole version available for private acquisition. This extreme scarcity commands a massive premium. Assuming the work is in good condition (pastels are fragile and light-sensitive, requiring strict conservation), and given its status as a 'blue-chip' asset of the highest order, a realistic estimate today would significantly exceed the 2012 hammer price. Factoring in inflation and its unique market position, a valuation in the range of $150 million to $160 million is appropriate. I have settled on $155,000,000 as a realistic fair market value.",
    "valuation_usd": 155000000,
    "confidence": 0.95,
    "key_factors": [
        "Global Iconography and Universal Recognizability",
        "Extreme Rarity (Only version in private hands)",
        "Aesthetic Superiority (Most colorful of the four versions)",
        "Historical Market Precedent ($120M sale in 2012)",
        "Pinnacle of Expressionist Art History"
    ]
}

parsed.internal_reasoning:
This artwork is undoubtedly Edvard Munch's 'The Scream,' specifically the 1895 pastel on board version. This identification is confirmed by the visual evidence: the intense, vibrant red and orange hues of the sky (distinct from the darker 1893 tempera version), the specific textural quality of the pastel strokes, and the signature 'E Munch 1893' in the lower left corner. It is a well-documented nuance that Munch dated this 1895 pastel '1893,' aligning it with the genesis of the composition, despite it being executed two years later. This version is widely considered by scholars and the market to be the most aesthetically striking of the four versions due to its vivid coloration and the expressive freedom afforded by the pastel medium.

Art-historically, this piece is a cornerstone of Expressionism and modern art. It captures the existential angst of the fin de siècle, translating internal psychological turmoil into external landscape distortion. The skeletal figure, the blood-red sky (often theorized to be inspired by the 1883 Krakatoa eruption), and the isolating perspective create an image of universal human anxiety. Its cultural resonance is unparalleled; it is likely one of the top five most recognizable images in global history, sitting alongside the Mona Lisa and Starry Night.

Regarding valuation, we must look at the specific market history of this object. This exact work (the 1895 pastel) was sold at Sotheby's New York in May 2012 for $119,922,500, setting a record for the artist and for any work of art at auction at that time. To arrive at a current fair market value, we must adjust for over a decade of inflation (roughly 35-40% cumulative since 2012) and the appreciation of 'trophy' assets. While the broader art market has seen fluctuations, the ultra-high-end sector for masterpiece Impressionist and Modern art has remained robust, with works by Modigliani and Picasso achieving prices in the $170M–$180M range in private and auction settings in the mid-2010s. 

Furthermore, the prompt notes this is the 'only one in private hands.' The other three versions are held by Norwegian institutions (The National Museum and The Munch Museum in Oslo), making this the sole version available for private acquisition. This extreme scarcity commands a massive premium. Assuming the work is in good condition (pastels are fragile and light-sensitive, requiring strict conservation), and given its status as a 'blue-chip' asset of the highest order, a realistic estimate today would significantly exceed the 2012 hammer price. Factoring in inflation and its unique market position, a valuation in the range of $150 million to $160 million is appropriate. I have settled on $155,000,000 as a realistic fair market value.
key_factors: Extreme Rarity (Only version in private hands)
Aesthetic Superiority (Most colorful of the four versions)
